unit.name: RL successes, sim-to-real transfer, and model-free limitations
unit.description: Uses games and robotics case studies to show what model-free reinforcement learning can achieve and why sample efficiency, safety, and interpretability remain major bottlenecks.
unit.summary: The segment illustrates RL’s strengths through case studies. In Breakout, the agent first learns reliable control and later discovers a non-obvious tunnel strategy that accelerates scoring [ts=1542s] [ts=1599s] [ts=1615s]. Li then sketches the progression from AlphaGo to AlphaGo Zero, AlphaZero, and MuZero to argue that simple scalable recipes and, later, learned latent dynamics can produce very strong game-playing systems [ts=1757s] [ts=1779s] [ts=1826s]. In robotics, he highlights sim-to-real locomotion results showing that imperfect simulations can still transfer if they are randomized enough, while noting that manipulation remains less mature and often unreliable [ts=1921s] [ts=1957s] [ts=2002s] [ts=2040s]. He closes by identifying model-free RL’s core weaknesses: massive interaction cost, safety issues, limited interpretability, and the absence of the predictive internal models humans use for planning [ts=2110s] [ts=2147s] [ts=2180s] [ts=2199s].

key_points.text: Breakout agents can discover a tunnel strategy that outperforms naive play after extended exploration.
AlphaGo-family systems show the value of scalable RL recipes, with MuZero adding planning through a learned latent dynamics model.
For locomotion, sim-to-real transfer can work well even when the simulator omits details like bushes or snow.
Robot locomotion is close to solved by reinforcement learning, but dexterous manipulation is still less reliable and less general.
Model-free RL needs extensive interaction, can be unsafe on real hardware, and is hard to interpret or correct when it fails.
Humans rely on predictive internal models of the world, motivating a shift toward learned models for planning.

kp_catalog.name: Model-free RL successes and sim-to-real limitations
kp_catalog.description: RL has produced dramatic wins in games and locomotion, yet still suffers from sample inefficiency, safety concerns, and limited robustness in many manipulation settings.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because model-free RL successes and sim-to-real limitations clarifies rL has produced dramatic wins in games and locomotion, yet still suffers from sample inefficiency, safety concerns, and limited robustness in many manipulation settings, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Reinforcement learning through trial and error
kp_catalog.description: Reinforcement learning trains an agent by repeatedly interacting with an environment and shifting behavior toward actions that produce higher return.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because reinforcement learning through trial and error formalizes how reinforcement learning trains an agent by repeatedly interacting with an environment and shifting behavior toward actions that produce higher return, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.
